questions.text: Which of the following are recommended strategies to reduce systemic toxicity from local anesthetic use during hysteroscopy? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: B) Using a vasoconstrictor like epinephrine
D) Choosing highly vascular injection sites
E) Using the lowest effective dose

questions.explanation: Avoid intravascular injection (aspiration), add epinephrine to reduce systemic absorption when appropriate, and use the lowest effective dose. Glycine is not used for anesthetic dilution; injecting into highly vascular sites increases systemic uptake.
questions.submodule: Module 7.1 – Complications and Dosing of Anesthetics

questions.text: Which 3 agents or techniques are commonly used to facilitate cervical preparation in premenopausal women undergoing operative hysteroscopy? Select 3 best answers.
questions.explanation: Misoprostol, osmotic dilators, and dilute vasopressin are standard options. Glycine has no role in ripening; balloons are for tamponade, not ripening.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about uterine perforation management are incorrect? Select all that are incorrect.

answerOptions.text: A) Fundal perforations with blunt dilation and stable vitals often require emergency surgery
B) Lateral wall perforations with bleeding require surgical exploration
C) Electrosurgery use during perforation increases risk of visceral injury
D) Hemodynamic instability always necessitates observation only
E) Cervical stenosis increases the risk of uterine perforation

questions.explanation: Expectant management is typical for uncomplicated fundal perforations (no energy, stable vitals). Instability requires surgical evaluation—not observation only. Lateral wall bleeding, energy use, and stenosis are risk/management factors as stated.

questions.text: Which 2 strategies are most effective in reducing the risk of systemic complications during vasopressin injection in hysteroscopy? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Injecting rapidly at 3 and 9 o’clock
D) Injecting directly through the cervical os
E) Administering 10 units per injection site in the cervical stroma

questions.explanation: Use dilute solutions and aspirate before injection to avoid intravascular delivery. Rapid or blind injections and high doses increase risk.

questions.text: Which of the following are characteristics of electrolyte-poor distention media used in hysteroscopy? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: B) Higher risk of hyponatremia if absorbed
D) Recommended maximum fluid deficit is 1500 mL
E) Should be avoided in renal insufficiency

questions.explanation: Electrolyte-poor fluids (e.g., glycine, sorbitol) are for monopolar systems, carry higher hyponatremia risk, and are avoided in renal disease. Typical deficit limit ≈1000 mL (not 1500 mL).

questions.text: Which 2 techniques reduce the risk of perforation when using a resectoscope in thin-walled uterine regions? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Slowly advancing the activated loop along base of target tissue
C) Applying pressure with the inactive loop to test for weakness
D) Resection with loop withdrawal under direct vision
E) Maintain fluid pressure above mean arterial pressure

questions.explanation: Cut on withdrawal under direct vision and add image guidance in high-risk areas. Advancing an activated loop, probing with the loop, or excessive pressure are unsafe.

questions.text: What is the earliest symptom of bupivacaine toxicity?
questions.explanation: Perioral numbness/metallic taste commonly precede CNS excitation and cardiovascular effects.
questions.submodule: Module 7.1 – Complications and Dosing of Anesthetics

questions.text: Which factor does NOT influence the risk of systemic local anesthetic toxicity?
questions.explanation: Dose, vascularity, and vasoconstrictor use matter; dispersive electrodes pertain to electrosurgery, not LAST.
questions.submodule: Module 7.1 – Complications and Dosing of Anesthetics

questions.text: What is the maximum dose of 2% lidocaine with epinephrine in a 70 kg adult?
questions.explanation: Max 7 mg/kg × 70 kg = 490 mg (2% = 20 mg/mL → ~24.5 mL).
questions.submodule: Module 7.1 – Complications and Dosing of Anesthetics

questions.text: Which of the following is a benefit of cervical preparation prior to hysteroscopy?
questions.explanation: Ripening softens/dilates the cervix, facilitating safer entry and reducing false passage/perforation.

questions.text: Which of the following agents is used for cervical ripening in premenopausal women?
questions.explanation: All listed are valid ripening options depending on timing and indication.

questions.text: A 43-year-old undergoes hysteroscopy, but resistance is met at the cervix and the procedure is abandoned. Which agent would best facilitate safe future entry?
questions.explanation: Prostaglandins and osmotic dilators are reasonable; vasopressin may aid hemostasis/entry. A tailored plan often uses one or more.

questions.text: What is the most common location for uterine perforation during hysteroscopy?
questions.explanation: The fundus is most frequently involved with dilators/scope insertion.

questions.text: Which of the following is mostly likely an indication for immediate surgical exploration following uterine perforation?

answerOptions.text: A) Fundal perforation with blunt instrument
C) Use of electrosurgical energy during perforation
D) Loss of distension without bleeding

questions.explanation: Energy at time of perforation raises risk of occult visceral/vascular injury—explore.

questions.text: During hysteroscopy, a perforation occurs at the left lateral wall with bleeding and hypotension. What is the next best step?

answerOptions.text: D) Use bipolar energy to achieve hemostasis

questions.explanation: Instability with bleeding requires operative evaluation (laparoscopy/laparotomy).

questions.text: What is a known benefit of cervical vasopressin injection during hysteroscopy?
questions.explanation: Vasoconstriction lowers intraoperative bleeding.

questions.text: Which of the following is a potential risk of cervical vasopressin use?
questions.explanation: Systemic absorption may cause hypertension and bradycardia.

questions.text: Which of the following practices helps reduce the risk of vasopressin-related complications?

answerOptions.text: B) Injecting more than 10 mL at a time
C) Drawing back before each injection
D) Rapid bolus injection into cervical os

questions.explanation: Aspirate prior to injection to avoid intravascular delivery.

questions.text: Which of the following is an electrolyte-poor distention medium?
questions.explanation: Glycine (and sorbitol/mannitol) are electrolyte-poor; saline/Ringer’s/PlasmaLyte are electrolyte-containing.

questions.text: Which distention fluid is appropriate for use with a monopolar resectoscope?
questions.explanation: Monopolar systems use non-electrolyte media (e.g., glycine, sorbitol, mannitol).

questions.text: In operative hysteroscopy using monopolar energy, when should the procedure be terminated due to fluid deficit?
questions.explanation: Electrolyte-poor media carry a lower deficit threshold (≈1000 mL) due to hyponatremia risk.

questions.text: What is the recommended direction of electrode movement when using a resectoscope?

answerOptions.text: B) Cutting away from the camera
C) Withdrawing motion over the pathology

questions.explanation: Standard technique is to cut on withdrawal under direct vision.

questions.text: Which uterine locations require extra caution during resectoscopy due to thin myometrium?

answerOptions.text: D) Cervical os and anterior wall

questions.explanation: Cornua and cesarean scars have thinner myometrium, increasing perforation risk.

questions.text: What is a key strategy to reduce perforation risk during resectoscopic surgery in high-risk locations?

answerOptions.text: A) Continuous advancement of the loop
B) Use of monopolar energy only
D) Rapid distension with CO₂ gas

questions.explanation: Adjunct guidance is recommended when anatomy is thin/distorted.
